hq: 169 Madison Ave STE 2457, New York, NY 10016

system_description.overview: Trellus is an AI-powered parallel dialer platform for sales teams. The platform provides end-to-end VoIP for outbound/inbound calls, AI detection of live humans on calls to automatically patch reps into live conversations, AI-powered practice bots (synthetic simulated prospects), and automated call analytics.

products.Telephony System: End-to-end VoIP solution for outbound calls and inbound call handling; AI detection of live humans to auto-patch reps
products.AI Practice Bots: Synthetic AI bots simulating prospects for rep training; automated grading and evaluation of practice calls
products.AI Call Analytics: Automated analytics to analyze and understand cold call performance
products.Live Coaching: Implementation of live coaching suggestions during cold calls

infrastructure.cloud_provider: AWS (carve-out — AWS controls excluded from scope)
infrastructure.physical_presence: None — no physical offices; all data processed on cloud services; operates from co-working space; environmental controls managed by building management / AWS
infrastructure.availability_zones: Multi-AZ (DR via spinning up multiple servers across all availability zones in AWS per Section 5)

network_architecture.network_segmentation: VPC (trellus-vpc) with segmented subnets and Access Control Lists (ACLs). Network diagram shows us-east-1 with trellus-vpc containing multiple named EC2 instances across subnets: trellus-subnet (private with 2 EC2 instances) and trellus-subnet-public1-us-east-1a (public subnet with ~20+ named EC2 instances: router, log1, service-1, machine1, relay, config, dashboard, engine1, service-2, service-17, machine-1 through machine-4, machine-x, machine-doc-x, machine-doc-u, machine-doc-1, machine-doc-4, tier-1). GitHub and Pro Service icons shown outside VPC. Customer access appropriately segmented from other customer accounts (CC6.1.3).
network_architecture.api_gateway: Not explicitly mentioned — direct EC2-based architecture inferred from network diagram
network_architecture.firewalls: System firewalls configured on application gateway and production network to limit unnecessary ports, protocols, and services. Firewall rules reviewed annually by IT management (CC6.6.1).
network_architecture.ids_ips: IDS and IPS via AWS to identify and protect against threats in conjunction with security policy network settings (CC6.6.1 confirmed)
network_architecture.vpn: Remote access via bastion host with VPN
network_architecture.tls: TLS via established third-party certificate authority for all web-based system requests (CC6.1.6)
network_architecture.content_filtering: Content filtering system routed through proxy server; all systems restricted to internet via content filtering
network_architecture.topology: Production runs in us-east-1, trellus-vpc. Multiple named EC2 instances in public subnet (router, log1, service-1 through service-17, machine instances, relay, config, dashboard, engine1, tier-1). Private subnet holds 2 additional EC2 instances (trellus-prime instances). DR via multi-AZ spinning up of servers. GitHub and Pro Service are external integrations. Raw EC2 architecture — no ECS, EKS, or Lambda detected.

application_architecture.pattern: Microservices on raw EC2 (inferred from 20+ named service instances in network diagram)
compute: EC2 (confirmed in network diagram — 20+ named instances: router, log1, service-1, service-2, service-17, machine1, machine-1 through machine-4, machine-x, relay, config, dashboard, engine1, tier-1, machine-doc instances)
application_architecture.api_style: Not specified (VoIP telephony suggests WebRTC or SIP protocol alongside REST)
application_architecture.environments: Separate development/test environment confirmed (CC8.1.4: changes developed and tested in separate dev/test environment before implementation)

databases: Not explicitly named — AWS-hosted database referenced
data_storage.backup_frequency: Not explicitly stated — backup policy documented; Backup Policy referenced

authentication_access_control.identity_provider: AWS (password policy via AWS — periodic forced changes, password expiry, complexity requirements, account lockout after limited failed attempts; Active Directory referenced for infrastructure accounts)
authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for user accounts with administrative access to production platform (CC6.1.1); source code management tool access requires MFA (CC8.1.3); email access requires 2FA
authentication_access_control.access_revocation_sla: Within one business day upon termination (CC6.1.4)
authentication_access_control.password_policy: AWS password policy: periodic forced changes, password expiry, complexity requirements, account lockout after limited failed attempts; guest/anonymous logins not allowed; unattended desktops locked on inactivity

encryption.in_transit: TLS (version not specified — 'encryption technologies used to protect communication and transmission of data over public networks')
encryption.at_rest: AWS KMS — 'organization uses its cloud provider key management service to encrypt data at rest and to store and manage encryption keys' (CC6.1.5)
encryption.key_management: AWS KMS (access to production environment's access keys restricted to authorized individuals)

ci_cd_devops.source_control: GitHub (inferred — source code management tool with MFA requirement and branch protection per CC8.1.2, CC8.1.3; GitHub icon visible in network diagram)
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Code repository branch rules configured to require additional approval for every merge request to move change to production environment (CC8.1.2)
ci_cd_devops.iac: Configuration management tool used for baseline configurations and rollback capability (specific tool not named — 'baseline configurations are retained within the infrastructure as a code and a configuration management tool is used for implementation and for rollback capability')
ci_cd_devops.container_orchestration: None — raw EC2 instances; no ECS/EKS/Kubernetes mentioned
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: CTO sets up automatic patch management; monthly patch management reviews; workstations scanned daily for patch compliance; OS patches applied automatically (CC7.5.1)

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: AWS-native monitoring (automated monitoring tool generates alerts when predefined thresholds are met — CC5.2.1)
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: Logging enabled for administrative activities, logon attempts, data deletions, changes to functions, security configurations, permissions, and roles (CC7.2.1)
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts configured to notify IT management of high and critical risk events; issues resolved via incident management process (CC7.2.1)

third_party_services.purpose: Primary cloud infrastructure — compute (EC2), networking (VPC, ACLs), multi-AZ deployment (us-east-1), key management (KMS)
Source code management with branch protection and MFA; visible in network diagram as external integration
External service integration — specific purpose not disclosed

security_tools.antivirus: Anti-virus and Malware protection software installed and configured on all production servers; continuous scanning; weekly AV definition updates for production servers; installed and configured for Mac-based workstations and laptops (CC6.8.1)
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications; management reviews and takes action on high/critical vulnerabilities; remediation plan developed for critical/high findings (CC7.1.2). Self-assessment scans prior to every production release or annually.
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annual external penetration testing by independent third party; high/critical findings remediated and tracked (CC4.1.1)

excluded_criteria: CC6.4 — Physical access to data center facilities: AWS is responsible for physical security; not in scope
A1.2 — Environmental protections: Trellus has no office; operates from co-working space; environmental controls managed by building management / AWS
Processing Integrity — Determined not relevant to the system
Privacy — Determined not relevant to the system
notable_controls: CC6.1.1: MFA enforced for user accounts with administrative access to production platform
CC6.1.4: Access revoked within one business day of termination; employee termination checklist used
CC6.2.2: Quarterly user access reviews for all accounts including privileged accounts
CC7.1.2: Monthly automated vulnerability scans; high/critical remediation plan required
CC4.1.1: Annual external penetration testing by independent third party
CC8.1.2: Branch protection enforced — merge to production requires additional approval
CC8.1.3: Source code changes logged, time-stamped, attributed to author; MFA required for SCM access
CC7.4.1: BCDR policy documented, reviewed annually, and tested annually with multiple loss scenarios
CC7.1.1: Baseline configurations retained as code with configuration management tool for rollback
Note: This is a Type 1 report (design assessment only, not operating effectiveness)

infrastructure.rationale: Single region (us-east-1) with multi-AZ. Raw EC2 architecture — the network diagram shows 20+ named EC2 instances directly in a public subnet, which is architecturally less mature than ECS/EKS and raises concerns about fleet management at scale. IaC referenced. No CDN or WAF. No container orchestration.

application_architecture.rationale: 20+ named EC2 instances suggest a distributed microservices-like deployment but on raw EC2 without container orchestration. VoIP telephony is architecturally complex (likely WebRTC/SIP). Branch protection and separate dev/test environment confirmed. CI/CD tool unnamed. Many instances in a public subnet is a security concern.

data_layer.rationale: Database engine not named. Backup frequency not disclosed. Data classification and retention policies referenced. Per-tenant segregation stated. No caching layer mentioned. Very limited technical specifics about the data tier. VoIP call audio and analytics data storage approach entirely undescribed.

security_posture.rationale: Annual external pen test, monthly vulnerability scans, MFA on admin and SCM access, quarterly access reviews, 1-business-day access revocation, IDS/IPS via AWS, antivirus on all servers and workstations, firewall rules reviewed annually. No WAF. No cybersecurity insurance mentioned. Many EC2 instances in public subnet is a surface area concern. No SIEM.

devops_maturity.rationale: Branch protection enforced, emergency change process, IaC for baseline configurations, separate dev/test environment, automatic patch management with monthly reviews. No container orchestration. CI/CD tool not named. Raw EC2 fleet management without orchestration is operationally heavier. GitHub visible in network diagram.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy documented and reviewed annually. Annual testing with multiple loss scenarios confirmed. Multi-AZ failover (spinning up multiple servers across AZs). No multi-region. No explicit backup frequency stated. RTO/RPO not disclosed. Section 5 BCDR is management assertion only — not audited.

vendor_diversity.rationale: Heavily AWS-concentrated with EC2, VPC, KMS. Only additional identifiable vendor is GitHub (inferred from network diagram) and an unnamed 'Pro Service' integration. Lowest vendor diversity in cohort — essentially single-vendor with AWS.

overall.rationale: Trellus is a VoIP/AI dialer platform with a technically notable but operationally complex raw EC2 architecture — 20+ named instances without container orchestration is unusual for a modern startup and suggests manual fleet management. SOC 2 Type 1 only (design, no operating effectiveness). Key risks: public subnet EC2 exposure, no WAF, no named CI/CD tool, no database specifics, no backup frequency, no cybersecurity insurance, and the telephony use case processes sensitive live call audio data. The multi-AZ deployment is positive but single-region.

red_flags: SOC 2 Type 1 only — design assessment, no evidence controls are actually operating effectively
20+ EC2 instances placed directly in a public subnet (trellus-subnet-public1-us-east-1a) — significant attack surface; unusual for a security-conscious architecture
No container orchestration (no ECS/EKS) — raw EC2 fleet management creates operational and security complexity
Database engine and backup frequency not disclosed
CI/CD tool not named — reduces confidence in deployment automation maturity
VoIP platform processes live call audio — sensitive data category not addressed in disclosed controls
yellow_flags: Auditor (Accorp Partners) is a lower-tier/newer SOC 2 audit firm — not Big 4 or well-known mid-tier
Controls table appears heavily templated — identical boilerplate language reused across multiple criteria (Accorp Partners pattern)
No SIEM despite logging and monitoring controls
Unknown 'Pro Service' vendor visible in network diagram — purpose and security posture undisclosed
Public brand (Trellus/trellus.ai) differs from legal entity name (Outpost Labs Inc.)
green_flags: Annual external penetration testing by independent third party
Monthly automated vulnerability scans with high/critical remediation plan
BCDR policy tested annually with multiple loss scenarios and business impact analysis
MFA enforced on admin production access, email, and source code management
Quarterly access reviews and 1-business-day access revocation SLA
AWS KMS for encryption at rest and in transit
Branch protection with required additional approval for merges to production
IaC baseline configurations with configuration management tool for rollback
key_observations: Trellus is a VoIP parallel dialer — the system processes live outbound/inbound call audio and AI-generated transcripts/analytics, making it a high-sensitivity data processor. Call recordings and analytics may constitute personal data subject to TCPA and state wiretapping laws

The network diagram shows 20+ named EC2 instances in a PUBLIC subnet (trellus-subnet-public1-us-east-1a) — instances named router, log1, service-1, machine1, relay, config, dashboard, engine1, service-2, service-17, and multiple 'machine' and 'machine-doc' instances are all directly internet-accessible, which is architecturally concerning

The VoIP telephony use case requires real-time audio processing which inherently demands low-latency, high-availability infrastructure — the lack of a CDN, WAF, or DDoS protection is more concerning for a telephony company than for a typical SaaS
The 'Pro Service' external integration visible in the network diagram is unexplained — could be a telephony carrier, CRM integration, or analytics provider

The org chart shows CEO, CTO, Engineering Team, Sales Team, and SEO Team — the presence of an 'SEO Team' is unusual for a B2B SaaS and may indicate the company is smaller than it appears with multiple functions compressed into few people

Ajinkya Nene (CEO) is the signing authority, not the CTO — in most SOC 2 reports for technical companies the CTO signs; this may indicate the CTO role is shared or that the CEO directly owns security decisions
BCDR section is explicitly labeled 'Other Information Provided by Trellus' not audited by Accorp — DR description is management assertion only
